steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Konno et al. claim that perceived vulnerability (linked to iris color) explains why participants preferred some dogs for interaction or keeping. The blank must supply specific data showing that less mature-looking dogs — the ones perceived as more vulnerable — were also preferred for interaction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘the more mature a dog was perceived to be, the more likely participants were to rate it as having light irises.’: links maturity to iris color rating, not to interaction preference. Does not illustrate the vulnerability-to-preference mechanism. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘participants favored the dogs in images 2 and 11, which they rated as less mature than the dogs in images 20 and 16.’: directly ties higher interaction preference (favored) to lower maturity ratings (less mature), illustrating that vulnerability-signaling dogs were more sought after. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘participants rated the dog in image 2 as less mature than the dog in image 11 and rated the dog in image 16 as less mature than the dog in image 20.’: compares maturity within the preferred and non-preferred groups but never mentions interaction preference. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘dogs that participants rated as friendlier were also dogs that participants indicated a stronger willingness to interact with or keep.’: substitutes friendliness for vulnerability, which is not the mechanism the researchers identified. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
